sentences.text: Yes.
You see, we were working at the Simmons factory with my older sister, Clara, and myself.
And my youngest sister, Elizabeth, and my cousin, [?
Elise ?], they worked in the premises in the lager.
They were making like small little spoons sort of wiring thing.
And what hurt me very much, that as a young girl she had to work during the night.
Now, for us it was very hard because the light was on in the barrack all night long.
We had to get up early in the morning, go to work.
The walking back and forth, and the noise.
You really had very little chance to sleep.
You had to be exhausted to fall asleep.
And by the time you would fall asleep, it was time to get up.
But we didn't mind that because the food was fairly good.
And we know that is no crematorium.
And we were taking day by day, and we were hoping that one of these days the war will be over.
It cannot be too long, because some of the civilian people who were overseeing the workers in the lager, they were letting go the few words that you will be liberated and so and so.
So we knew that it can-- cannot be too long.
Yes.
We were there till the beginning of March.
But the meantime, we went through a lot of bombing.
Day and night, day and night.
If not the Americans, the Russians.
They were dropping the Stalin candle, we called it, that lighted up the whole area.
And then we were rushed to the bunker, to the-- to the shelter.
The Lageralteste said, don't put the blanket over your head because they will think that you are soldiers.
Just put the blanket on your shoulder and go that way.
And that was one occasion when I realized that by going to the bunker, that's the best time to steal potatoes.
Because we had the potato [HUNGARIAN] next to our barrack.
So I would ran to the [HUNGARIAN].
I would put the few potatoes into my coat pocket.
Then I would run with the other girls to the-- to the shelter.
At one occasion, I had taken a few potatoes from the-- from the [HUNGARIAN].
And I was running.
But by that time, all the girls went into the-- to the shelter.
So I was banging on the door, and two of these [GERMAN] opened the door.
And they gave me a beating, and they kicked me under my knee, that I fell on my nose.
They said that I fall asleep, and I'm a lousy lazy this and this and this and this.
So I-- finally I crawled out and I went to the other entrance of the-- of the shelter.
Over there, the [GERMAN] opened the door and gave me a hit, and I went in.
And this happened quite often.
But what happened this particular time, the [GERMAN] that had kicked me and gave me a beating and called me all kinds of lazy thing, when the bomb was coming we felt it that it's going to hit us.
Because when you have so many [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] days and nights of experience with the bombing, your ear gets so used to it you know already how far the bomb is dropping.
And that particular time, we felt that the way the wind, the pressure, was coming, that we are being hit.
But we didn't know where or how.
We came out of the shelter.
We were lined up.
The bomb had hit right at the front of the entrance where the [GERMAN] had kicked me and chased me away.
And those two [GERMAN] were in pieces.
They were dead.
That some of the Jewish girls already took the cigarette out of their uniform pocket.
They were taking whatever they could find in their pocket.
They were taking out.
When we went upstairs on the-- on the ground, the [GERMAN] was counting us, and counting us at least 50 times.
He didn't want to believe it, that none of us-- but none of us were dead from the half-lings except the two German [GERMAN].
From then on, whenever there was a bombing or anything, the [GERMAN] and the-- and the soldier, they would always come to near us because they said that God is protecting you.
They were mean and they were heartless, but they were afraid for their life.
Then they believed in God, when it was concerning their life.
That was one occasion.
Another occasion, I went to steal some potatoes.
And I seen this girl from the kitchen who was taking potatoes to the kitchen.
And they were nobody around.
So I said to her, let me take a few potatoes.
She started cursing, and she gave me a kick almost the same place where the German woman gave me the kick.
And I fell on my face.
I got so disgusted.

I said to her, you know, for one Jewish girl to the other, I said, you have the nerve to kick me because I asked you for the potatoes, and-- I said, God will punish you, that you going to break your leg.
Sure enough-- I mean, not that I want to sound big, that I had predicted that she going to break her-- she was walking on the ice and she did break her leg.
So when I met her, I said, you see?
I told you you're going to break your leg.
I said, you were so easy to kick me and to-- to push me.
I said, now God punish you.
You see?
So that was one occasion.
Were there any health problems among you?
Yes.
Yes.
I was getting to it.
But my older sister, Clara-- I was very sick in Auschwitz.
And my younger sister, Elizabeth, the skinnier she was, my cousin-- as skinny as she was-- they never were sick.
My older sister developed such a fever that her teeth was going like-- like a machine gun.
So she said she's freezing.
She's freezing.
So I went, I laid down naked.
She laid on top of me.
And my younger sister Elizabeth laid on top.
But I couldn't breathe.
I said, please.
I can't do that.
So then we lined up sideways and we put the blanket on her.
And then finally, she fell asleep.
We had no aspirin.
We had no hot tea.
We had nothing.
So what happened is that I had seen that these girls are bringing the bucket in from the-- from the-- [HUNGARIAN] in from the kitchen.

So I wanted to dig in like I had seen it in Auschwitz that they were doing it, because here it wasn't that many people walking around, because most of the people were inside in the barrack because it was wintertime.
It was cold.
So whom I see, the steel and ugly looking black face woman, the one who was traveling with us in the wagon from Auschwitz, who wanted the knife, but she didn't want to give me the slice of bread.
And there was a beautiful blonde woman with her, and they were carrying the [HUNGARIAN].
So I picked up the top and I dig in the bucket.
I mean, the pot.
And she said you get away from me.
You this and that.
So I said to her, you know, if you gonna holler-- she said, I'm going to holler.
I'm going to call the Germans.
So I said to her, if you want to holler, I said, this beautiful face you have, I said, you're not going to have it anymore.
I said, I'm going to punt this paint-- pot of soup right to your face.
And I mean it.
She said, she's crazy.
She's crazy.
So the black haired says, oh, she's the one from the-- from the wagon.
Oh, she's mean and she's this and that.
And meantime, I took the soup.
And as I'm walking into the barrack, who is coming up said me, the-- the-- the [GERMAN], the head of the [GERMAN].
I didn't know what to do.
So I put the pot between my leg.
And it was burning me, but burning me until she pass by, because you had to stand erect to give them respect.
And after she left, I took the bucket out and I was holding it underneath my dress.
I walked in, and we were drinking it, eating it, together what we were getting.
It was easy to mix it up.
So then later on, I went over to this blonde woman, and I said to her, I have a young sister who is very skinny.
That's the reason I dig into the bucket.
And I would like to give you my portion of the liverwurst, that all four of us-- we were getting a slice liverwurst like this, round.
And I said, I give you the liverwurst, but let me dig into the bucket, to the-- to the [HUNGARIAN].
This black face-- haired woman says that she is a liar.
She's a no good.
Don't believe a word what she said.
I said to the blonde one, I said, look.
I'm telling you the honest truth.
I wouldn't lie to you.
She says to me, you know what?
Bring here your sister.
If I see your sister, then I believe you.
So I bring over my sister, Elizabeth.
And I said to her, this is my sister.
So she looks at her, and she starts to cry.
So I said to myself, she can't be such a bitch.
I mean, if-- if she could still cry after Auschwitz, she still has a heart.
So she says to me, I could never take the liverwurst from you because if I take it and she dies, I will never forgive myself.
I will always blame myself for the rest of my life, providing that I will live.
No.
She said, any time if you could dig into the bucket, I will let you dig into the [HUNGARIAN].
I said, thank you very much.
I said, you are a real friend.
I said, you have a heart.
And from then on, she wasn't any more carrying the [HUNGARIAN].
Somehow they relieved her.
So we became very good friends.
And she was telling me stories about her brothers, her sisters, her family.
I was telling her about my family, and so on.
I introduce her to my other sister, my cousin.
And one day, she comes to me, that I'm very hungry.
And I'm together with my sister.
Then she told me that this black haired woman who was so mean and cursing, that she's her husband sister.
That that's her sister-in-law.
And that she has another sister-in-law with her, and the cousin of the sister-in-law's.
Her husband cousin.
So they were four of us, and we were four of us.
So she said, they are very hungry.
And how can I get some food?
So I said to her, you know what?
I figured it out already.
I was thinking about this.
That when everybody goes to sleep, I will put-- my sister Clara would put the blanket front of the window, because we had windows on the-- on the barrack.
And the windows were covered with shutter, with wooden shutter that when they were bombing or so, so that the light shouldn't go out.

So I figured it out that if my sister Clara and Elizabeth would hold the blanket over the window, and I would crawl under it, and then I would push the shutter open, providing I listen that when the snow is not crunching, that the German man, the soldier, is not walking by.
Then I could sneak out and going to the potato [HUNGARIAN].
While we were talking, another person overheard us.
And she said, I want to be in it.
I said to her, no.
Because three person is unlucky.
Two person could go and do it.
She insisted that she will tell the [GERMAN] if I don't let her come.
I said to her, OK.
You could come under one condition.
If any one of us caught, do not tell that there was how many people or what's the name of it.
They going to kill you anyway, this way or that way.
So doesn't make any difference.
Don't involve the other one.
She promised she won't do it.
So we went out.
We ran over to the [HUNGARIAN].
The German guard was at the other end of the barrack.
And as I mentioned, I got this raincoat from Auschwitz which had the lining.
And God bless the tailor, whoever made it.
He made it so strong, the seams, that I was able to put in potatoes all the way up to my waistline.
And this other woman, the blonde, she had the towel what we receive when we arrive to Nuremberg, they sewed it up, and she made a bag out of it.
This other woman, I don't know what she had.
The only thing I know, she was so overjoyed with the potatoes that when she packed herself up, she didn't listen.
She didn't watch.
She was going back to the barrack.
And sure enough, it was dark.
It was like drizzling, sort of.
She went-- she went right smack into the German soldier.
When I heard that-- and she started screaming.
So the-- the German was-- the German soldier was screaming and hollering.
And he was grabbing her, and taking-- taking her back to the barrack.
So when I heard that, so we ran away from the potato barrack.
And not far from there, there was a building that halfway finished-- we were building it because we couldn't go to the Simmons factory anymore so we were building it there.

The matter of fact, prior to that, there was an incident where we were digging the [HUNGARIAN] for the potatoes, and there were these big, husky Italian prisoners, and they were building the building.
And as I'm standing there in the-- in the gutter, or in the ditch, he's passing by.
And he had his Italian uniform on him.
And he looked at me.
And he starts to shake himself.
I get so scared.
What is he shaking?
And as I look down, the beautiful boiled potatoes is dropping out.
So I hurriedly picked it up.
And then he gave a big smile.
I said, my God.
This is a miracle.
And one day, when we had a big air raid, as the bomb were coming, I know it is coming nearby, that they were living next to us.
A little fence had divided the-- the two lager.
Nothing was left of them except a wooden shoes, which I picked up.
Because by that time my shoes was going, and so and so.

So what happened is that when we were stealing the potatoes, and from the [HUNGARIAN], after that they caught the girl, or she was stupid enough to run into the soldier, we ran into this building what they were building, these Italians were building.
My sister-in-law emptied her potato.
She didn't have not one potatoes on her.
We hear the screaming and screaming.
Quieted down.
Then we see that they coming out, and they are nailing in every shutter.
Whatever shutter was there, every shutter was nailed in except the bathroom.
Because the bathroom, from the cold and the body heat, the steam formed an ice near the windows so they couldn't close the shutter.
They couldn't close the window either.

So as I'm sitting there freezing off, I was thinking that if my sister Clara is smart, she will used the wooden shoes-- because the wooden shoes had a nails sticking out at the heel so that when you walk, you walk like you are limping.
And if he-- she will use the nail from the heel of this wooden shoes, then she will able to chop up the ice.
But is easier said now than it was that time.
First of all, everybody was looking if any strange movement is going on.
And the Stubenalteste were the bigger bastard you ever want to encounter with.
They-- they love to snitch on you.
They love to tell bad things about you.
So what happened, my sister had to watch when the Stubenalteste wouldn't watch, and nobody else was in the lavatory-- in the washroom.
So she kept on chopping the ice.
When she came back, she gave the shoes to my sister Elizabeth.
She was chopping.
Meantime, when I was outside, I was thinking exactly the same thing that they will do.
So when it-- I was praying that this should be a alarm, that the bomb should come, the plane should come.
The icy rain was coming.
And we are sitting there.
And then what happened, the [GERMAN] comes with a tremendous big heavy flashlight, like a searchlight.
And he's going all through the [HUNGARIAN] and everything.
And then as he is facing the light towards us, I said, dear God.
My mother.
Please help me.
And I fainted.
I didn't know, you know-- he didn't find us.
Now, my sister-- I mean, my-- my friend kept on saying, my dear mother, please pray for us.
Help us.
I will never steal again.
So I said to her, you are a stupid idiot.
You say you're not going to steal again.
Tomorrow, you're going to be hungry again.
You're going to steal again.
How could you say that you're not going to steal again?
We didn't get the air raid.
There were no bombing, no plane coming, and we were getting very morning already.
So I said to this friend of mine, we have to try to see if the washroom window is open.
So sure enough, when the soldier went to the other end, we took a dash.
Because the washroom was sort of like towards the end.
So when he walked up that way, we had a chance to run to the window.
So we go there.
Sure enough, it opened up.
And I pushed my friend through.
Then I was going.
And being that I had so many potatoes in my pocket, in my lining, I fell in head first.
It broke the ice, the-- the-- the drain.
And my head get all wet and everything.
So finally, we were inside already.
I close the shutter.
We go in tiptoe, that nobody should see us.
Which is a miracle nobody did.
Then when I climb up on the bed, my sisters said, thank God that you are here already.
So I said to her, we have to change coat.
And I have to empty the potatoes somewhere, because I'm sure there will be a [GERMAN].
When is a [GERMAN], they used to line you up outside Zahlappell.
Come in, empty everything, the whole place, upside down.
So meantime, there was one toilet that was not working properly.
And didn't make any different for the girls.
They went there anyway.
So the extreme was this high.
So I figured it out the best thing is if I climb through the-- because it was divided.
So I climb through.
I will place the potatoes on top of the extreme, which was frozen anyway.
So it wasn't that it was fresh.
And when the Zahlappell comes, I won't have potatoes on me.
So it was-- it was-- it was much earlier than usually, we had the Zahlappell.
We all go out.
And they made us kneel down.
We were kneeling on-- on our knees, and they counting and counting and counting and counting.
Nobody is missing.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] Meantime, to get back to this girl whom the German had caught, they were beating her to such an extent that she made a ciss under herself.
And then one of the [GERMAN], after she beat her, she says to her, why did you steal?
Why did you have the nerve to go out, and how did you go out?
She said, I didn't go out.
I was left out.
I was hiding all-- all-- all night long.
She didn't want to say that-- how she went out.
So the German [GERMAN] says that being that you don't want to snitch on the other people-- because don't tell me-- you didn't do this alone.
I'm going to bring you some soup.
She went to the kitchen.
She brought soup for her.
When-- when I came back and my sister told me what happened, I said, they are crazier than we are.
First, she beats her to-- almost to death.
Then she brings her a plate of soup.
We were in Nuremberg from November till March, which about four-- good four months.
But in that four months, we were going through a lot of bombing, a lot of suffering.
And then-- I have to finish this.
I'm sorry, Ellen, dear.
But what happened is after that-- the Zahlappell, we went inside.
And as I'm walking in, I change coats with my sister, Clara.
This Lola, the Lageralteste from Auschwitz, she gave me a slap that I thought my head going to fell off.
How did she know that I was it?
It must be my face, that I was frozen up and so on.
So after that, I climbed back on the toilet.
I took out the potatoes.
I counted it.
It was 99.
How it fit into my coat, I don't know.
So I gave 33 potatoes to the woman who was caught and beaten.
I gave 33 potatoes to the blonde one.
And we had 33.
And I felt very good about it, that I was able to do that.
But previous to that, there was an incident in Nuremberg where one of the woman who was married before-- she was very rich in Budapest-- she stole one of our blankets.
Because what they did in Nuremberg, they took the blankets apart, and they took sticks from the street, and they were knitting.
They were making all kinds of things to cover the stomach, to keep them warm.
So she went and she took the blanket.
My sister went and said to her, give me back my blanket.
I saw you when you took it.
She said, I didn't take your blanket.
You are a liar.
So she went and she-- my sister took the blanket, took it back.
And she had a little scab on her face.
She took off the scab, and the blood was coming out.
She went over to this Lola, because she spoke perfect German.
And she said the four [GERMAN] stole my blanket.
So when Lola heard that, she said, four-- the-- [GERMAN].
Line up.
So we lined up.
She took one of this wisp broom, you know?
And she made us turn our dress over.
So when I saw what she wants to do, I said to her-- now, she said, I'm going to give 25 to each one of you for hurting the woman.
So I said to her, we didn't hurt the woman.
She is lying.
No.
So I said to her, OK.
Give me 50, and don't give any to my younger sister.
She said, you are such a good [GERMAN].
You going to get the 50, but she going to get the 25.
That hurt me so, that how can she be such a bastard?
To-- to-- what satisfaction that she get out of it?
I'm sorry about the language, but she wasn't-- she was worse than that.
So what happened is that she was giving the-- the 25 to each one of us, but she wasn't made of iron either.
So in the beginning, it really was very powerful.
It hurt, everything.
But then in the end, it-- it wasn't so bad.
So we were get-- getting bombed day in, day night.
We had no more barrack.
The barrack was bombed down.
The kitchen was bombed down.
So we were staying for the whole month underground in the shelter.
So what happened, some of the girls-- we were very hungry because we didn't get food for the first week.
We didn't get water.
We didn't get food.
So we went up from the shelter and we were eating the snow.
After a while, the girls were very hungry.
So they ran to the [HUNGARIAN], the potato [HUNGARIAN], and they were trying to steal potatoes.
Some of the German soldiers were shooting them like pigeons.
One girl, a young girl, her knees were shot to pieces.
Another woman, an elder woman whom I befriended with her-- with her niece, her behind-- you know, the heavy meat?
The bullet went in.
My sister Clara meantime fainted, because she thought that if it's stealing, I will be there for sure.
Meantime, I went to the kitchen.
And under the-- the big pot, I was collecting some clean potatoes that they were doing-- cooking for the soldiers.
And I had quite a bundle of it.
So when I went back, she is fainted.
I said to her, I'm here.
I'm alive.
Don't faint.
I'm all right.
I wasn't there.
I wasn't among them.
I said, but I have potatoes.
Just to keep quiet.
So that was one incident.
And the woman came to me whose behind was shot, that I should take out the bullet because I have the knife.
To take it out.
I looked at it.
I could see the bullet, but I just didn't have the heart.
Because I said that-- what I'm going to do if it's going to get infected, and then she going to blame me.
I said to her, they going to take the girl with the-- with the knee.
You go and you tell the [GERMAN] that you have a bullet in your behind.
Then the-- so the meantime, they went to the hospital.
They took out the bullet.
The girl with the shot up knee, she-- she remained in the hospital.
And this girl came back.
She was with us.
Many of times, they had asked us to go and do errand in the city with this German [GERMAN].
And when we were walking-- we went to a bakery once, and I couldn't steal anything.
So I said, that's a waste.
Once, they asked me to volunteer.
I volunteered.
They took one of the girl who died.
I had to take her to the cemetery that was across the street from the lager.
And we had to lay the body down into one of these cement four.
I never seen something like that.
I don't know what they did with her.
We left her there.
And I said never again.
I'm not going to volunteer, because this is not for me.
